op.name: floor_divide
op.summary: Return the largest integer smaller or equal to the division of the inputs. It is equivalent to the Python ``//`` operator and pairs with the Python ``%`` (`remainder`), function so that ``a = a % b + b * (a // b)`` up to roundoff.
op.signature: we.floor_divide(x1, x2, /, out=None, *, where=True, casting='same_kind', order='K', dtype=None, subok=True[, signature])

blocks.text:
Return the largest integer smaller or equal to the division of the inputs.
It is equivalent to the Python ``//`` operator and pairs with the
Python ``%`` (`remainder`), function so that ``a = a % b + b * (a // b)``
up to roundoff.

desc_blocks.text:
Denominator.
If ``x1.shape != x2.shape``, they must be broadcastable to a common
shape (which becomes the shape of the output).

items.name: out
items.type: ndarray, None, or tuple of ndarray and None, optional

desc_blocks.text:
A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have
a shape that the inputs broadcast to. If not provided or None,
a freshly-allocated array is returned. A tuple (possible only as a
keyword argument) must have length equal to the number of outputs.

This condition is broadcast over the input. At locations where the
condition is True, the `out` array will be set to the ufunc result.
Elsewhere, the `out` array will retain its original value.
Note that if an uninitialized `out` array is created via the default
``out=None``, locations within it where the condition is False will
remain uninitialized.

For other keyword-only arguments, see the
:ref:`ufunc docs <ufuncs.kwargs>`.

y = floor(`x1`/`x2`)
This is a scalar if both `x1` and `x2` are scalars.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.floor_divide(7,3)
2
>>> np.floor_divide([1., 2., 3., 4.], 2.5)
array([ 0.,  0.,  1.,  1.])

blocks.text:
The ``//`` operator can be used as a shorthand for ``np.floor_divide``
on ndarrays.
divmod — Simultaneous floor division and remainder.
floor — Round a number to the nearest integer toward minus infinity.
ceil — Round a number to the nearest integer toward infinity.
